content.text: Keeping a database schema synchronized across every environment — local dev, CI, staging, and production — is one of the most error-prone parts of deploying software. Schema migrations solve this by encoding every structural change to a database as a versioned, executable file that teams commit alongside their application code, apply deterministically, and reverse when something goes wrong.

A schema migration is a discrete, ordered unit of work that alters a relational database's structure: creating or dropping tables, adding or removing columns, building indexes, enforcing constraints, or seeding reference data. Each migration is stored as a file (Python, SQL, or TypeScript, depending on the tool) and given a unique revision identifier. A dedicated table in the database — commonly called
— records which revision is currently applied, so the tooling knows exactly how far the schema has advanced and which revisions still need to run.
The two canonical functions inside every migration are upgrade
. The upgrade function applies the change forward; the downgrade reverses it. Each migration declares its predecessor so the tool can build a directed chain from the initial empty schema to
applies every unapplied revision in order, regardless of how many steps behind the database is.
Migrations are purely declarative about intent: "add a nullable column

". The migration tool translates that declaration into dialect-appropriate DDL — the exact SQL that Postgres, MySQL, or SQLite requires. This abstraction means the same Python migration file works across supported databases without hand-written SQL differences.

Agentic AI systems evolve continuously. A new capability — parallel tool execution, token-cost tracking, human-in-the-loop approval gates — almost always requires new tables, new columns, or new indexes. Without a structured migration workflow, schema drift accumulates: the developer's local database looks nothing like production, CI tests pass against a subtly different schema, and deployments break because the running code expects columns that do not exist yet.

The moment a required column is missing on production, every request that touches that column raises an exception. If the column was added manually to dev and staging but never scripted, the omission is only discovered when the feature goes live. Schema migrations prevent this by treating the database structure as version-controlled source code: no change is valid until it exists as a committed migration file that CI applies automatically.

Agent products compound this need. A single agent run can involve dozens of database writes — run metadata, tool call events, approval records, retry counts, token usage. The schema evolves nearly as fast as the agent's capabilities, and multiple engineers often work on agent features in parallel. Without migrations, concurrent schema edits collide silently; with them, conflicts surface as merge conflicts in migration files, which are reviewable and resolvable before any code ships.
— Every environment — dev, CI, staging, production — arrives at the identical schema by replaying the same ordered revision chain.
— Migration files live in git: every change is attributed to a commit, a PR, and a review. Teams can see exactly who introduced a column and why.
— When a flawed migration reaches production, running downgrade reverses the structural change while a fix is prepared.
— Alembic compares ORM model definitions to the live database and generates the migration diff automatically, reducing hand-written DDL errors.
— CI gates integration tests behind upgrade head, guaranteeing tests never run against a stale schema.
— Migration files are code: they go through the same PR review process as application logic, catching schema mistakes before they reach production.
— Teams co-develop database features independently; migration chains surface conflicts at merge time, not at deploy time.

— Alembic's autogenerate detects column additions, type changes, and index changes but misses renames (it treats them as drop + add, losing data). Generated output must always be reviewed and sometimes rewritten.

— On tables with tens of millions of rows, adding a NOT NULL column or building an index can lock the table for minutes. Zero-downtime strategies require multi-step migrations spanning multiple deploys.

— Downgrade functions are rarely exercised outside dev. A downgrade that worked locally may fail on production data that does not match dev fixtures, leaving the database in a partially-reverted state.
Data migrations are a separate concern
— Backfilling existing rows with new column values requires careful batching to avoid locking or overwhelming the database. This is error-prone and separate from the structural migration.

— When multiple engineers create migrations from the same head, the revision chain branches. Both files must be committed with a merge migration or one rebased on the other before CI can run them in order.
— Once a migration is applied to production it is effectively immutable. Editing it creates a checksum mismatch; the fix is always a new forward migration.
Alembic — the de-facto migration tool for Python/SQLAlchemy projects — represents the revision chain as a singly-linked list. Each revision file contains a
identifier (a short hex string) and a
pointer. When you run upgrade head, Alembic reads the

table to find the current position, traverses the chain forward collecting every revision between current and head, then executes their upgrade functions in sequence inside a transaction (by default).
file in the Alembic project configures how the tool connects to the database and where it imports your ORM metadata from. When you invoke
, Alembic introspects the live database schema and compares it against the ORM's metadata (the set of

definitions in your models). It then generates a new revision file containing only the diff — the precise operations needed to bring the database from its current state to the state the ORM describes.

# migrations/versions/abc123_add_token_counts.py

"""add token_counts column to runs

Revision ID: abc123
Revises:     abc122
Create Date: 2026-07-10 09:14:33
"""

from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

revision = "abc123"
down_revision = "abc122"
branch_labels = None
depends_on = None


def upgrade() -> None:
    op.add_column(
        "runs",
        sa.Column(
            "token_count",
            sa.Integer(),
            nullable=True,
            comment="Total tokens consumed by this agent run",
        ),
    )
    # Back-fill existing rows with 0 so callers get a non-null value
    op.execute("UPDATE runs SET token_count = 0 WHERE token_count IS NULL")
    op.alter_column("runs", "token_count", nullable=False)


def downgrade() -> None:
    op.drop_column("runs", "token_count")
table contains a single row with one column,
. Its value after the migration above would be

. Alembic uses an advisory lock or serializable transaction to prevent two concurrent upgrade commands from applying the same revision twice — a critical safety guarantee in multi-instance deployments where several pods start simultaneously.
By default each revision runs in a transaction: if upgrade raises an exception, the entire migration rolls back and
remains unchanged. Some DDL operations (e.g. Postgres CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY) cannot run inside a transaction. For those, set
in env.py and manage transactions manually inside the migration function.

The diagram below shows the full lifecycle of a migration from authoring to production. Developers generate a revision file, commit it alongside feature code, and CI applies the migration to an ephemeral test database before running integration tests. The same pipeline later applies to staging and production, each of which may lag behind by one or more revisions. The revision chain at the bottom shows how each migration links back to its predecessor.

attrs.alt: Schema migration pipeline showing developer → migration file → git → CI/CD → Dev/Staging/Production databases with revision chain

content.text: Use schema migrations for every structural change to a relational database — no exceptions. Whether adding a column for a new feature, renaming a table for clarity, or adding an index for a slow query, the change must exist as a committed migration file before it ever runs on any environment that matters. Manual SQL executed directly on production is dangerous because it bypasses version control and review, and it is forgotten the moment the DBA session closes.
— A new agent feature requires storing per-run token costs. Adding a column to the runs table must be a migration so every developer, CI job, and production host applies it identically.
— A high-traffic table needs an index on run_status to avoid full scans during the agent's polling loop. A migration with CONCURRENTLY avoids a full table lock.
— A tool_events table is renamed to agent_events. A migration handles the rename and updates foreign key constraints atomically.
— A multi-tenant platform must populate a tenant_id column on all existing rows after adding it. The migration adds the column (nullable), and a subsequent data migration backfills rows in batches.
When not to use migrations directly
— Large data transformations (moving millions of rows between tables) belong in standalone idempotent scripts with batching, not inside a migration that holds a lock for the entire operation.
— Schema-less stores such as Redis or document databases like MongoDB do not use SQL DDL; use application-level versioning or document schema validators instead.

pip install alembic sqlalchemy psycopg2-binary

# Inside your project directory:
alembic init migrations

# This creates:
#   alembic.ini          ← connection URL goes here
#   migrations/
#     env.py             ← imports your ORM metadata
#     versions/          ← migration files live here

# migrations/env.py  (relevant excerpt)
from myapp.models import Base          # your SQLAlchemy declarative base
from myapp.settings import DATABASE_URL

config.set_main_option("sqlalchemy.url", DATABASE_URL)

# Tell autogenerate which tables to track
target_metadata = Base.metadata

# Autogenerate from ORM diff
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "add tool_events table"

# Or write it by hand (for complex changes autogenerate can't express)
alembic revision -m "create_partitioned_runs_table"
Step 4 — Review and edit the generated file

Always open the generated file before running it. Check for renames represented as drop + add (data loss), check that foreign key names are correct across dialects, and verify that the downgrade function is complete and reversible.

# Apply all pending migrations
alembic upgrade head

# Check current revision
alembic current

# Show full revision history
alembic history --verbose

# Apply one step at a time (useful for debugging)
alembic upgrade +1

# .github/workflows/ci.yml  (relevant excerpt)
steps:
  - name: Run migrations
    run: alembic upgrade head
    env:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost/testdb

  - name: Run integration tests
    run: pytest tests/integration
table used to audit every tool call an agent makes during a run. It includes an index on

# migrations/versions/abc122_add_tool_events.py
from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

revision = "abc122"
down_revision = "abc121"


def upgrade() -> None:
    op.create_table(
        "tool_events",
        sa.Column("id", sa.BigInteger(), primary_key=True, autoincrement=True),
        sa.Column(
            "run_id",
            sa.UUID(as_uuid=True),
            sa.ForeignKey("runs.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
            nullable=False,
        ),
        sa.Column("tool_name", sa.String(64), nullable=False),
        sa.Column("input_json", sa.Text(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column("output_json", sa.Text(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column("status", sa.String(16), nullable=False, server_default="ok"),
        sa.Column(
            "created_at",
            sa.DateTime(timezone=True),
            server_default=sa.text("now()"),
            nullable=False,
        ),
    )
    op.create_index("ix_tool_events_run_id", "tool_events", ["run_id"])


def downgrade() -> None:
    op.drop_index("ix_tool_events_run_id", table_name="tool_events")
    op.drop_table("tool_events")

This pair of migrations demonstrates the expand-contract pattern for adding a NOT NULL column to a large table without locking it. The first migration adds the column as nullable and starts the backfill; the second sets the constraint once every row has a value. Splitting across two deployments means the running application is never stuck waiting for a long-running DDL lock.

# migrations/versions/abc123a_add_approval_nullable.py
"""Step 1/2: add approval_required as nullable (expand)"""
revision = "abc123a"
down_revision = "abc122"

from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

BATCH_SIZE = 5_000


def upgrade() -> None:
    # Add column as nullable — no table lock, instant on large tables
    op.add_column(
        "runs",
        sa.Column("approval_required", sa.Boolean(), nullable=True),
    )
    # Backfill in batches to avoid a single massive UPDATE
    conn = op.get_bind()
    while True:
        result = conn.execute(
            sa.text(
                "UPDATE runs SET approval_required = FALSE "
                "WHERE approval_required IS NULL "
                "LIMIT :batch"
            ),
            {"batch": BATCH_SIZE},
        )
        if result.rowcount == 0:
            break


def downgrade() -> None:
    op.drop_column("runs", "approval_required")

# migrations/versions/abc123b_set_approval_not_null.py
"""Step 2/2: tighten approval_required to NOT NULL (contract)"""
revision = "abc123b"
down_revision = "abc123a"

from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa


def upgrade() -> None:
    # At this point every row has a non-null value; constraint is safe
    op.alter_column("runs", "approval_required", nullable=False)


def downgrade() -> None:
    op.alter_column("runs", "approval_required", nullable=True)

An agentic-AI platform serving thousands of concurrent agent runs needed to add per-run cost tracking for billing. The runs table had 120 million rows and received 50,000 inserts per minute. A naive ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN NOT NULL would have locked the table for several minutes — unacceptable downtime.
The team applied the expand-contract pattern across three sequential releases:
and starts an async backfill job that updates rows in batches of 10,000 with a small sleep between batches to avoid saturating I/O.
Once the backfill job signals completion (via a metadata flag), a second migration runs
ALTER COLUMN cost_usd SET NOT NULL
. The application code already writes the column on every new run, so no lock is held waiting for a full-table write.
A cleanup migration adds a check constraint

Total production impact: zero downtime, no locked reads, and the billing feature shipped two weeks after the schema planning started. The entire migration sequence is recorded in git, reviewed in PRs, and can be replayed identically if the team ever rebuilds the database from scratch.

# async_backfill.py — run as a one-off job between Release 1 and Release 2
import asyncio
import asyncpg

BATCH = 10_000
SLEEP_S = 0.05  # 50 ms pause between batches


async def backfill():
    pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(dsn="postgresql://...")
    async with pool.acquire() as conn:
        while True:
            rows = await conn.execute(
                """
                UPDATE runs
                SET cost_usd = 0
                WHERE cost_usd IS NULL
                LIMIT $1
                """,
                BATCH,
            )
            updated = int(rows.split()[-1])
            print(f"Backfilled {updated} rows")
            if updated == 0:
                break
            await asyncio.sleep(SLEEP_S)
    print("Backfill complete")


asyncio.run(backfill())

Once a migration file has been applied to any shared environment, its content is frozen. Editing it causes Alembic to detect a checksum mismatch and refuse to proceed. The correct action is always to create a new forward migration that corrects the earlier one.

# WRONG — do not edit abc122 to add a column you forgot
# vim migrations/versions/abc122_add_tool_events.py  ← NO

# RIGHT — create a new revision to add the missing column
alembic revision -m "add missing status_detail column to tool_events"
Adding NOT NULL without a default on a large table

-- WRONG: locks the entire table while Postgres rewrites every row
ALTER TABLE runs ADD COLUMN priority INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;

-- RIGHT: add nullable first, backfill, then set constraint
ALTER TABLE runs ADD COLUMN priority INTEGER;         -- instant
UPDATE runs SET priority = 0 WHERE priority IS NULL; -- batched externally
ALTER TABLE runs ALTER COLUMN priority SET NOT NULL;  -- fast: no rewrite
Dropping a column still read by deployed code
If version N of the application reads column

and you drop it in the migration deployed with version N+1, any pod still running version N crashes with a column-not-found error during the rolling deploy. The safe sequence: deploy version N+1 that stops reading the column → wait for all old pods to terminate → run the drop migration.
Skipping the review of autogenerated output
appears as a drop of tool_name followed by an add of tool_slug — a destructive data-loss operation. Always open and read every generated file before running it, even in development.
— Each migration should express one logical change with a concise, descriptive message. A message like "add_approval_required_to_runs" is far more useful than "schema_update_v3".
— Store migrations in the same repository as application code. PR reviewers who see the ORM model change should also see the migration file in the same diff.
— Add a CI step that runs alembic upgrade head against a fresh database and then runs all integration tests. A migration that breaks integration tests fails before merge.

— For columns that will eventually be NOT NULL, use the expand-contract pattern: nullable add → backfill → constraint. Never add NOT NULL with a server_default on a table that already has data unless the table is provably empty.
Always build indexes concurrently on large tables
— Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on Postgres for all index additions on non-trivially sized tables. This requires a non-transactional migration (transaction_per_migration = False).

— Take a verified backup before applying any migration to production. For managed databases (RDS, Cloud SQL), trigger a manual snapshot in the deploy runbook and record its ID before the migration step.
— Test the downgrade path in staging before every production deploy. A broken downgrade is only a problem during an emergency rollback, which is exactly the worst time to discover it.
— Document the required deploy order in the PR description and deploy runbook: migrate then roll API then roll workers. Some migrations must precede code; others must follow.

Most schema migrations on small tables (under a million rows) complete in milliseconds and impose no meaningful performance burden. The risk appears at scale: tables with tens or hundreds of millions of rows, heavy write loads, or complex constraint graphs.

In Postgres, adding a column with a volatile DEFAULT causes a full table rewrite — every row is physically rewritten with the new column value. For a 100 million-row table this can take 20–40 minutes and holds an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock the entire time. Postgres 11+ avoids the rewrite for constant defaults (an integer literal), but the lock is still held momentarily at the start and end of the statement.
Standard index creation on Postgres acquires a ShareLock that blocks writes for the duration. For an agent-platform

table receiving thousands of inserts per second, this can mean minutes of write queueing. CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY builds the index in multiple phases without blocking writes, at the cost of taking roughly twice as long and being interruptible. Always prefer CONCURRENTLY on live tables.

When a Kubernetes rolling deploy starts multiple new pods simultaneously, each pod may attempt alembic upgrade head on startup. Without a coordination mechanism, two pods can try to apply the same migration concurrently. Alembic uses
on the version table to serialize migration runners — only one process proceeds while others wait. Verify that your database user has the necessary lock permissions.

Alembic is the standard migration tool in Python environments that use SQLAlchemy. It supports autogeneration, custom migration scripts, multi-database deployments, and non-transactional DDL. Most FastAPI, Flask, and Django-with-SQLAlchemy projects use it.

pip install alembic sqlalchemy
alembic init migrations
alembic revision --autogenerate -m "describe change"
alembic upgrade head
alembic downgrade -1
alembic history --verbose
alembic current
Django's built-in migration system generates migration files from model changes automatically via
. It is tightly coupled to the Django ORM and handles model inheritance, ManyToMany tables, and data migrations (RunPython operations) natively.

python manage.py makemigrations agents --name add_token_count
python manage.py migrate
python manage.py showmigrations
python manage.py sqlmigrate agents 0004  # preview the SQL
Flyway uses plain SQL files named by convention (e.g.,
) and tracks applied migrations in a
table. It is language-agnostic and works with any JDBC-compatible database. A popular choice for Java Spring Boot applications and multi-language monorepos.
Prisma Migrate generates SQL migration files from changes to a
model file. It stores applied migrations in
and integrates with the Prisma ORM client. Suitable for TypeScript-first agent backends using Node.js or Bun.

# TypeScript / Node.js with Prisma
npx prisma migrate dev --name add_token_count
npx prisma migrate deploy        # apply in CI / production
npx prisma migrate status        # check pending migrations
A lightweight Go library that reads numbered SQL files (
) and applies them via a simple CLI or Go API. Supports Postgres, MySQL, SQLite, and many others. Preferred in Go services where adding an ORM dependency is undesirable.
Several approaches exist for managing database schema evolution. The table below compares schema migrations against the most common alternatives.

Migrations require DDL privileges (CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE, DROP COLUMN) which are much broader than the SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE privileges the application normally uses. Run migrations under a separate
database user, not the application user. This prevents a SQL injection vulnerability in the application from escalating to schema destruction.

Never hardcode connection strings, API keys, or tenant-identifying data inside migration files. Migration files are committed to version control and often included in build artifacts. Use environment variables and inject them at runtime via
A migration that fails partway through (network blip, disk full, timeout) may leave the database in a partially-applied state. Write migrations that tolerate re-execution where possible: use

guards for CREATE TABLE and CREATE INDEX, and wrap destructive steps in existence checks. Alembic's transactional mode rolls back on failure by default, but DDL-outside-transaction migrations need manual idempotency.
Tables that record agent actions (tool calls, approvals, token usage) should be append-only: the application user gets INSERT but not UPDATE or DELETE. Enforce this at the database level:

Add a health check that compares the running application's expected migration head against the database's current revision. If the database is behind, the health check returns degraded, allowing the load balancer to hold traffic until the migration completes.

from alembic.runtime.migration import MigrationContext
from alembic.script import ScriptDirectory
from sqlalchemy import engine_from_config

def check_migration_health(config) -> dict:
    engine = engine_from_config(config)
    script = ScriptDirectory.from_config(config)
    with engine.connect() as conn:
        context = MigrationContext.configure(conn)
        current = context.get_current_revision()
        head = script.get_current_head()
        up_to_date = current == head
    return {"status": "ok" if up_to_date else "pending", "current": current, "head": head}
Every schema change — column, table, index, constraint — must be a committed migration file. Manual DDL on any shared environment is a reliability risk.
The revision chain is an ordered, linked list. Alembic walks it from the database's current revision to head, applying each upgrade function in sequence.
Always read autogenerated migration files before running them. Renames appear as destructive drop + add operations and must be corrected manually.
Use the expand-contract pattern for zero-downtime column additions on large tables: add as nullable, backfill in batches, then enforce the NOT NULL constraint.
Build indexes with CONCURRENTLY (Postgres) to avoid locking writes on live agent tables. This requires a non-transactional migration.
Run migrations under a dedicated DDL-privileged user, separate from the application user, to limit the blast radius of any exploit.
Integrate migrations into CI: gate tests on a successful upgrade head, and verify the output section file is non-empty to catch silently-skipped malformed files.
Test the downgrade path in staging before every production deploy. A broken downgrade path is only discovered during an emergency rollback — the worst possible moment.
